6: The catalogue of Manasseh’s abominations вҖ” child sacrifice in Hinnom valley, divination, omens, sorcery, mediums and necromancers вҖ” reflects a systematic incorporation of Assyrian vassal-cult practices alongside indigenous Canaanite religion. The Assyrian Empire regularly imposed elements of its religious system on vassal states: Neo-Assyrian documents from the 8th-7th century BCE show that vassal treaties required acknowledgment of the Assyrian gods and often installation of their symbols. Manasseh’s 55-year reign (the longest of any Judahite king) coincided with the peak of Assyrian power under Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal; the Assyrian annals mention Manasseh as a vassal king who provided materials for building projects in Nineveh. The sДҒrim (servants of the host of heaven, v3) reflects the Assyrian ЕЎamГӘ u erб№Јeti (heaven and earth) oath-formula; the installation of altars to the host of heaven in the two courts of the temple (v5) is the visual marker of Assyrian religious hegemony in the Jerusalem cult. 2 Chronicles 33:11-13 records a tradition of Manasseh’s deportation to Babylon and repentance that the Kings account omits вҖ” the theological verdict in Kings (2 Kings 24:3: ‘because of the sins of Manasseh’) is the historian’s assessment of the long-term covenant damage that even a repentant Manasseh could not undo.
1: Manasseh’s regnal formula: twelve years old when he became king, reigning fifty-five years in Jerusalem (697вҖ“642 BC). His fifty-five year reign is the longest of any Davidic king вҖ” more than twice Hezekiah’s twenty-nine years. The irony is severe: the son of Judah’s greatest reforming king reigns longer than any other king and undoes everything his father accomplished. His co-regency with Hezekiah began around 697 BC, explaining the chronological overlap. Ancient Near Eastern parallels for such long reigns (Tiglath-pileser III, Esarhaddon) show that Manasseh ruled as a vassal of Assyria through the reign of Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal вҖ” his longevity partly reflects political accommodation to Assyrian overlordship.
2: WayyaКҝaЕӣ hДҒrДҒКҝ bбөүКҝГӘnГӘ YHWH kбөүtГҙКҝбөүбёҮГҙt haGGГҙyim вҖ” he did evil in the sight of YHWH according to the abominations of the nations. The specific reference to the nations YHWH drove out before Israel (КҫДғЕЎer hГҙrГ®ЕЎ YHWH mippбөүnГӘ bбөүnГӘ yiЕӣrДҒКҫД“l) is the standard judgment phrase for the most severe apostasy in Kings. Manasseh is measured not against previous Israelite kings but against the Canaanite standard вҖ” the baseline of paganism that Israel’s conquest was meant to displace. He reverses not just his father’s reform but the entire Deuteronomic purpose of the conquest.
3: The reversal of Hezekiah’s reform is total and explicit: Manasseh rebuilt (wayyiбёҮen) the high places his father had demolished, erected Baal altars, and made an Asherah as Ahab king of Israel had done. The double comparison вҖ” undoing Hezekiah and imitating Ahab вҖ” brackets Manasseh between the worst king of Israel and the best king of Judah, placing him emphatically on the wrong side. He also worshipped kol б№ЈбөүбёҮДҒКҫ haЕЎЕЎДҒmayim (all the host of heaven) вҖ” the astral cult condemned in Deuteronomy 4:19 and 2 Kings 17:3. The astronomical/astrological religion was Assyrian in character; Manasseh’s adoption of it reflects cultural assimilation to his overlords.
4: Manasseh builds altars in the temple itself вҖ” bбөүбёҮГӘt YHWH. The placement of Baal altars inside the temple of YHWH is the most extreme form of syncretism: not simply worship of other gods alongside YHWH but the introduction of pagan cult objects into the sacred space that represented YHWH’s exclusive dwelling. The narrator inserts the divine speech about the temple вҖ” КҫДғЕЎer КҫДҒmar YHWH bГ®rГ»ЕЎДҒlayim КҫДҒЕӣГ®m Кҫet ЕЎбөүmГ® (where YHWH said, in Jerusalem I will put my name) вҖ” precisely to heighten the sacrilege: the space of the divine name is profaned.
5: Altars to all the host of heaven in both courts of the temple. The two courts вҖ” the inner court of the priests and the outer court of the people вҖ” represent the full extent of the sacred precincts. To fill both courts with astral altars is to erase the distinction between sacred and profane throughout the temple complex. The host-of-heaven cult (Babylonian/Assyrian ЕЎar ЕЎamГӘ) was a sophisticated astral religion practiced throughout the ancient Near East; Manasseh’s adoption of it integrates the Jerusalem temple into a cosmopolitan imperial religious framework that explicitly displaced YHWH’s singular claim.
7: The placement of the carved Asherah pole (pesel hДҒКҫДғЕЎД“rДҒh) inside the temple вҖ” in the specific space about which YHWH had spoken to David and Solomon (КҫДғЕЎer КҫДҒmar YHWH Кҫel dДҒwid wбөүКҫel ЕЎбөүlЕҚmЕҚh bбөүnГҙ) вҖ” is the theological nadir. The Asherah, a female fertility deity associated with Baal, is placed in the space where YHWH said his name would dwell forever. The narrator invokes the Davidic covenant promise precisely at this point of maximum violation: the space of the covenant is given to the covenant’s antithesis. The tradition in 2 Chronicles 33:11-13 records that Manasseh was later taken captive to Babylon and repented вҖ” not mentioned in Kings, which focuses on the national rather than personal dimension.
8: YHWH’s conditional promise about the land: lЕҚКҫ КҫГҙsГ®p lбөүhДҒniКҝ regeL yiЕӣrДҒКҫД“l min hДҒКҫДғбёҸДҒmДҒh вҖ” I will no longer cause Israel’s feet to wander from the land I gave their ancestors вҖ” provided they keep all that I commanded them, all the Torah Moses commanded. The condition (raq Кҫim yiЕЎmбөүrГ») connects the land promise to covenant faithfulness. The narrator places this conditional promise here as the standard against which Manasseh’s failure is measured: the promise was clear; the condition was known; the failure was inexcusable.
9: wбөүlЕҚКҫ ЕЎДҒmбөүКҝГ» wayyatбөүКҝД“m mбөүnaЕЎЕЎeh laКҝДғЕӣГҙt rДҒКҝ mikkol haGGГҙyim вҖ” but they did not listen; Manasseh led them astray to do more evil than all the nations YHWH had destroyed before the Israelites. The comparison exceeds that of v2: not merely acting like the nations but exceeding them in wickedness. The verb tДҒКҝДҒh (to lead astray, to wander) combines the ideas of deliberate misleading and aimless wandering вҖ” Manasseh actively caused Israel to abandon its covenantal path. The moral trajectory of Judah is now downward beyond any previous low point.
10: The prophetic speech formula: waydabbД“r YHWH bбөүyaбёҸ КҝДғбёҮДҒбёҸДҒyw hannбөүбёҮГ®КҫГ®m вҖ” YHWH spoke by the hand of his servants the prophets. The phrase “by the hand of” (bбөүyaбёҸ) is the standard idiom for prophetic mediation вҖ” the prophet is the instrument through which YHWH speaks, not the originator of the word. The shift from historical narrative to prophetic speech marks a theological turning point: history has reached the moment where YHWH must announce its outcome.
11: The indictment: Manasseh has done more evil than the Amorites (haКҫД•mЕҚrГ®) who preceded Israel in the land. The Amorites were the generic designation for pre-Israelite Canaanite populations (cf. Genesis 15:16 вҖ” “the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete”). That Manasseh has now surpassed the Amorite standard of wickedness signals that the conditions that justified the original conquest are now reproduced within Israel itself вҖ” making Judah subject to the same judgment by the same logic. He has also caused Judah to sin (wayyaбёҘДғб№ӯГ®Кҫ Кҫet yбөүhГ»бёҸДҒh) with his idols вҖ” collective guilt through royal leadership.
12: YHWH’s announcement: hinnбөүnГ® mД“бёҮГ®Кҫ rДҒКҝДҒh Кҝal yбөүrГ»ЕЎДҒlayim wГ®hГ»бёҸДҒh вҖ” I am about to bring disaster on Jerusalem and Judah. The particle hinnбөүnГ® (behold me) marks a divine first-person announcement of imminent action. The expression КҫДғЕЎer kol ЕЎЕҚmбөүКҝДҒyw tiб№Јб№ЈalnДҒh ЕЎбөүtГӘ КҫoznДҒyw вҖ” both ears of whoever hears it will tingle вҖ” is a unique idiom for news of catastrophic scale (cf. 1 Sam 3:11 where the same expression describes the announcement of Eli’s house’s end). The disaster will be so severe that the mere report of it will physically affect the listener.
13: The measuring line of Samaria and the plumb line of the house of Ahab will be stretched over Jerusalem: wбөүnДҒб№ӯГ®tГ® Кҝal yбөүrГ»ЕЎДҒlayim КҫД“t qaw ЕЎЕҚmrГҙn wбөүКҫД“t miЕЎkЕҚlet bГӘt КҫaбёҘКҫДҒбёҮ. The measuring-line image (used in Amos 7:7-9 for the plumb line of judgment) applies to Jerusalem the same standard of destruction used against Samaria and Ahab’s dynasty вҖ” both now destroyed. The image of wiping and turning over a dish вҖ” wбөүmДҒбёҘГ®tГ® Кҫet yбөүrГ»ЕЎДҒlayim kaКҫДғЕЎer yimбёҘeh Кҫet haqqбөүКҝДҒrГ h вҖ” is vivid household imagery: Jerusalem will be wiped clean like a dish and turned upside down, emptied of its contents.
14: YHWH will abandon the remnant of his inheritance and give them into the hand of their enemies. The phrase ЕЎбөүКҫД“rГ®t naбёҘДғlДҒtГ® (the remnant of my inheritance) applies the language of covenantal possession to what will be relinquished. Israel was YHWH’s portion (naбёҘДғlДҒh); abandoning the remnant means withdrawing the covenant protection entirely. The enemies will plunder and despoil them вҖ” the passive role that YHWH had formerly taken as warrior-deliverer is now assigned to foreign powers against Israel.
15: The cause stated in full: yaКҝan КҫДғЕЎer КҝДҒЕӣГ» Кҫet hДҒrДҒКҝ bбөүКҝГӘnДҒy wayyihyГ» makkКҝГ®sГ®m КҫЕҚtГ® min hayyГҙm КҫДғЕЎer yДҒб№ЈбөүКҫГ» КҫДғбёҮГҙtГӘhem mimmitsrayim вҖ” because they have done evil in my sight and have provoked my anger from the day their ancestors came out of Egypt until today. The judgment is not sudden but the culmination of a long history of provocation beginning at the exodus. The Exodus is the starting point of the covenant relationship; rebellion has been continuous since its inception. Manasseh’s reign is not the beginning of Israel’s unfaithfulness but its decisive tipping point.
16: Manasseh sheds innocent blood (бёҸam nДҒqГ®) filling Jerusalem from end to end вҖ” mippeh laКҫpeh (mouth to mouth = from end to end). The innocent blood motif carries legal and theological weight: innocent blood cries from the ground (Genesis 4:10; Deuteronomy 19:10-13) and defiles the land, requiring expiation. The tradition that Manasseh executed the prophet Isaiah by having him sawn in half (reflected in Hebrews 11:37) is ancient and consistent with this notice of filling Jerusalem with innocent blood. The scale вҖ” filling the city вҖ” suggests systematic persecution of the faithful, not merely individual executions.
17: The standard regnal summary: Manasseh’s other acts and all he did, and the sin he committed, are recorded in the chronicles of the kings of Judah. The notation of “the sin he committed” (waбёҘaб№ӯб№ӯДҒКҫб№ҜГҙ КҫДғЕЎer бёҘДҒб№ӯДҒКҫ) as a separate archival category alongside his other acts treats Manasseh’s apostasy as defining enough to require specific mention in the official records вҖ” not just what he did but specifically his sin. The 2 Chronicles account (2 Kings 33:11-20) fills in the record of his captivity, repentance, and restoration, which the Kings narrator omits in order to maintain the judgment framework.
18: Manasseh’s burial in the garden of his palace, the garden of Uzza вҖ” not in the city of David (the royal necropolis). The departure from royal burial tradition (1 Kings 2:10; 2 Kings 11:43; etc.) signals either spatial constraint in the royal cemetery or deliberate exclusion. The garden of Uzza appears also in v26 for Amon’s burial. Burial outside the Davidic necropolis may reflect the theological judgment of the narrator: the king who desecrated the temple is buried outside the ancestral space of the covenant kings.
19: Amon’s regnal formula: twenty-two years old, reigned two years (642вҖ“640 BC). The brevity of his reign contrasts maximally with his father’s fifty-five years. Amon’s mother Meshullemeth is from Jotbah вҖ” an otherwise unknown location, possibly in Galilee or the Negev. The short reign follows the pattern of corrupt kings whose dynasties YHWH truncates; Amon’s assassination after two years is the natural consequence of continuing Manasseh’s apostasy without Manasseh’s longevity.
20: Amon did evil in YHWH’s sight as his father Manasseh had done. The evaluation is the most condensed possible вҖ” he is entirely identified with his father’s apostasy. The two-verse evaluation (v20-22) uses three different verbs for his religious failure: he did evil (rДҒКҝ), walked in the path of his father (wayyД“llek bбөүбёөol hadderek КҫДғЕЎer hДҒlaбёө КҫДҒбёҮГ®w), and served the idols his father had served (wayyaКҝДғбёҮЕҚбёҸ Кҫet haggillГ»lГ®m). GillГ»lГ®m (idols, literally “dung pellets”) is one of the most contemptuous terms in the prophetic/Deuteronomic vocabulary for pagan cult objects.
21: Amon walked entirely in his father’s path and served the same idols. The narrative’s economy here is deliberate вҖ” Amon receives minimal individual characterization, functioning as a structural marker: the apostasy of Manasseh continues uninterrupted into the next generation, confirming that it is not merely personal but dynastic and national. The specific detail wayyishtaбёҘГ» lДҒhem (he bowed down to them) adds the posture of active worship to mere toleration of idols.
22: The fundamental failure: wayyaКҝДғzЕҚбёҮ Кҫet YHWH КҫelЕҚhГӘ КҫДғбёҮЕҚtДҒyw wбөүlЕҚКҫ hДҒlaбёө bбөүdereбёө YHWH вҖ” he abandoned YHWH the God of his ancestors and did not walk in the way of YHWH. The language of abandonment (КҝДҒzaбёҮ) and the failure to walk in YHWH’s way inverts Deuteronomy’s core command (Deuteronomy 5:33; 2 Kings 8:6; 2 Kings 10:12). The reference to “the God of his ancestors” (КҫД•lЕҚhГӘ КҫДғбёҮЕҚtДҒyw) invokes the patriarchal covenant that Amon has abandoned вҖ” the ancestral relationship that defined Israel’s identity is severed by deliberate walking away from YHWH.
23: Amon’s assassination: his servants (КҝДғбёҮДҒбёҸДҒyw) conspired against him and killed him in his own palace. The assassination of a king by his household officials is the most intimate form of political violence вҖ” those closest to royal power become its destroyers. Amon’s murder in his own house (bГӘtГҙ) mirrors the vulnerability of dynasties built on apostasy. The co-conspirators’ identity and motive are not given; the brevity reflects the narrator’s focus on the theological trajectory rather than the political details.
24: The popular response: Кҝam hДҒКҫДҒreб№Ј (the people of the land) killed all who had conspired against Amon and made his son Josiah king in his place. The “people of the land” are the landowning class of Judah вҖ” a socially conservative force that had acted similarly after Athaliah’s usurpation (2 Kings 11:14-20). Their killing of the conspirators and installation of the legitimate heir shows the persistence of Davidic legitimacy in popular consciousness even after Manasseh’s apostasy. Josiah, though only eight years old, is the people’s choice as the continuation of the Davidic line.
25: The regnal summary notice for Amon: his other acts are written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah. The archive reference is even briefer for Amon than for Manasseh вҖ” his reign was too short to generate much administrative record. The summary closes the Amon episode with maximum efficiency, pivoting the narrative toward Josiah.
26: Amon’s burial: in his tomb in the garden of Uzza (the same garden as Manasseh’s burial, v18). Like his father, Amon is buried outside the Davidic city-of-David necropolis. The dynasty’s burial outside the royal ancestral ground continues; the geography of burial reflects the theological displacement of both kings from the covenantal norm. His son Josiah becomes king вҖ” the transition line that begins the chapter of Judah’s last great reform before the inevitable end.
